title: Assigning to an optional should directly target the optional
description: The class std::optional<T> either stores a value of type T or is empty. One way to access the value of a non-empty optional is the operator*. But using the dereference operator gives the optional appearance of a pointer when it is not: it models an object.
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

noncompliant:
std::optional<int> opt = 42;
*opt = 100; // Noncompliant: dereferencing to assign

std::optional<std::string> name = "John";
*name = "Jane"; // Noncompliant: treating optional like a pointer
compliant:
std::optional<int> opt = 42;
opt = 100; // Compliant: direct assignment to optional

std::optional<std::string> name = "John";
name = "Jane"; // Compliant: assign directly to optional

checks.message: Assigning to an optional should directly target the optional - avoid dereferencing for assignment.
